title: Why we trust handwriting more than print
summary: Argue why handwritten text — signatures, letters, ledger entries — continues to carry a weight of authenticity that print and digital text struggle to replicate.
topic: Why we trust handwriting more than print
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) on why handwritten text continues to carry greater perceived authenticity than printed or digital text, despite being less legible and slower to produce. Refer to at least one concrete example (e.g. the continued use of handwritten ledger entries to authenticate land transactions at Lima's Registro de Predios, or the role of handwritten signatures in international treaty law). Use a cleft sentence, a concession, a participle clause, and at least one passive construction. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What the continued use of handwritten ledger entries in Lima's Registro de Predios reveals is not bureaucratic inertia but…
Granted, the efficiency case for digital records is difficult to argue against…
Having encoded a person's physical movements into every loop and stroke, handwriting carries something that a typed font cannot…
A signature is not trusted because it is beautiful or legible but because it is…

bị giả mạo
sinh trắc học

hintVocab.phrase: to be indexed to the body
hintVocab.meaningVi: được gắn liền với cơ thể / được liên kết với cơ thể người
mang tính cá nhân độc đáo / đặc trưng riêng
hiệp ước

không thể tái tạo / không thể sao chép
chuyên gia giám định tài liệu pháp y
bị làm cho đồng nhất / bị chuẩn hóa
cử động vận động tinh tế (của bàn tay)
được giữ trong sự tin tưởng / được ký thác
có sức nặng / có tầm quan trọng
Cơ quan đăng ký bất động sản (Peru) — nơi đăng ký quyền sở hữu đất đai
được chứng nhận và ký tên

sampleText:
At Lima's Registro de Predios — Peru's land title registry — property transactions have been authenticated by handwritten ledger entries for over a century, and the practice persists alongside digitised records because the two are not considered equivalent. A digital entry can be altered without leaving a physical trace. A handwritten entry, amended in ink, shows every correction as a visible scar. What this asymmetry reveals is not a preference for inefficiency but a recognition that handwriting carries something print cannot: a record of a specific body in a specific moment.

Having encoded every tremor, pressure point, and habitual loop into its strokes, handwriting becomes a kind of biometric deposit. Forensic document examiners can identify a signature by the speed at which it was produced, the angle at which the pen was held, and the microscopic variations that no two people reproduce in quite the same way. This is precisely why signatures — not typed names, not digital tokens — remain the legally required form of consent on contracts, wills, and international treaties in most jurisdictions. The body is made to vouch for the mind's intention.

Granted, the argument for digital authentication has grown substantially stronger. Cryptographic signatures, biometric scans, and blockchain verification now offer levels of security that no handwritten document can match. A forged signature can be constructed by a skilled counterfeiter; a cryptographic key is orders of magnitude harder to replicate. The efficiency gains are real, and the risk of human error in handwritten records — misread names, smudged dates, transposed numbers — is well documented.

Even so, the persistence of handwriting in legal and ceremonial contexts suggests that what we want from authenticity is not only security but presence. A typed letter and a handwritten one may carry identical words, but they are not received identically. The handwritten version arrives as something made, not produced — a record of time spent, pressure applied, and attention given. When the British government's Instrument of Surrender was signed in Tokyo Bay in September 1945, the signatures of eleven officials were required in a handwritten form that no digital stamp could have replicated.

What we trust in handwriting is not its legibility but its irreproducibility. Print renders everything uniform; handwriting refuses to. In a world increasingly populated by generated text, that refusal may turn out to be among the most valuable properties any document can possess.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: What-cleft to name what handwriting carries that print cannot
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What this asymmetry reveals is not a preference for inefficiency but a recognition that handwriting carries something print cannot' — cấu trúc what-cleft bác bỏ một giải thích sai ('không phải sự ưa thích cho sự kém hiệu quả') trước khi đưa ra kết luận thực sự, tạo ra sự tương phản đanh thép.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What this asymmetry reveals is not X but Y' uses a what-cleft with 'not … but …' to dismiss the obvious explanation before presenting the real one at sentence-final position.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Perfect participle clause encoding prior cause
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'Having encoded every tremor, pressure point, and habitual loop into its strokes, handwriting becomes a kind of biometric deposit' — cụm phân từ hoàn thành nén lý do trước vào đầu câu, cho phép kết luận ('becomes a biometric deposit') xuất hiện tự nhiên hơn là qua mệnh đề 'because it has encoded …'.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'Having encoded … into its strokes, handwriting becomes …' uses a perfect participle clause to compress the prior cause, making the conclusion feel earned rather than stated.

sampleTranslationVi:
Tại Registro de Predios của Lima — cơ quan đăng ký quyền sở hữu đất đai của Peru — các giao dịch bất động sản đã được xác thực bằng các mục nhập sổ cái viết tay trong hơn một thế kỷ, và thực hành này vẫn tồn tại cùng với các hồ sơ số hóa vì hai loại không được coi là tương đương. Một mục nhập kỹ thuật số có thể bị thay đổi mà không để lại dấu vết vật lý. Một mục nhập viết tay, được sửa đổi bằng mực, hiển thị mọi sửa chữa như một vết sẹo có thể nhìn thấy. Điều mà sự bất cân xứng này tiết lộ không phải là sự ưa thích cho sự kém hiệu quả mà là sự thừa nhận rằng chữ viết tay mang điều gì đó mà in ấn không thể: hồ sơ của một cơ thể cụ thể trong một khoảnh khắc cụ thể.

Sau khi đã mã hóa mọi run rẩy, điểm áp lực và vòng lặp quen thuộc vào các nét bút, chữ viết tay trở thành một loại ký gửi sinh trắc học. Các chuyên gia giám định tài liệu pháp y có thể nhận dạng một chữ ký bằng tốc độ mà nó được tạo ra, góc độ mà bút được cầm, và những biến thể cực nhỏ mà không có hai người nào tái tạo hoàn toàn giống nhau. Đây chính xác là lý do tại sao chữ ký — không phải tên gõ, không phải token kỹ thuật số — vẫn là hình thức đồng ý được yêu cầu theo luật đối với các hợp đồng, di chúc và hiệp ước quốc tế ở hầu hết các khu vực pháp lý. Cơ thể được bắt phải bảo đảm cho ý định của tâm trí.

Công nhận, lập luận ủng hộ xác thực kỹ thuật số đã trở nên mạnh mẽ hơn đáng kể. Chữ ký mật mã, quét sinh trắc học và xác minh blockchain hiện cung cấp mức độ bảo mật mà không có tài liệu viết tay nào có thể sánh được. Chữ ký giả mạo có thể được tạo ra bởi một kẻ làm giả có kỹ năng; khóa mật mã khó sao chép hơn nhiều bậc độ lớn. Lợi ích hiệu quả là thực tế, và nguy cơ sai sót của con người trong các hồ sơ viết tay — tên bị đọc sai, ngày tháng bị lem, số bị đổi chỗ — được ghi chép đầy đủ.

Dẫu vậy, sự bền bỉ của chữ viết tay trong các bối cảnh pháp lý và nghi lễ gợi ý rằng những gì chúng ta muốn từ tính xác thực không chỉ là bảo mật mà còn là sự hiện diện. Một bức thư đánh máy và một bức thư viết tay có thể mang những từ giống hệt nhau, nhưng chúng không được nhận như nhau. Phiên bản viết tay đến như là thứ gì đó được tạo ra, không phải được sản xuất — hồ sơ của thời gian bỏ ra, áp lực được áp dụng và sự chú ý được trao. Khi Văn kiện Đầu hàng của chính phủ Anh được ký tại Vịnh Tokyo vào tháng Chín năm 1945, chữ ký của mười một quan chức được yêu cầu ở dạng viết tay mà không có con dấu kỹ thuật số nào có thể sao chép được.

Điều chúng ta tin tưởng ở chữ viết tay không phải là khả năng đọc được của nó mà là tính không thể sao chép của nó. In ấn làm cho mọi thứ đồng nhất; chữ viết tay từ chối điều đó. Trong một thế giới ngày càng được lấp đầy bởi văn bản được tạo ra, sự từ chối đó có thể trở thành một trong những thuộc tính có giá trị nhất mà bất kỳ tài liệu nào có thể có.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a what-cleft to dismiss a false explanation and name the real one?
options: What this asymmetry reveals is not a preference for inefficiency but a recognition that handwriting carries something print cannot.
This asymmetry reveals what is not inefficiency but recognition that print cannot carry handwriting.
It is asymmetry that reveals what handwriting carries and print cannot: not inefficiency but recognition.
What reveals this asymmetry is recognition not inefficiency — handwriting carries print cannot.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What this asymmetry reveals is not X but Y' uses a what-cleft with 'not … but …' to reject the obvious reading and state the real one at sentence-final position.
mcQuestions.hint: The correct cleft pattern is 'What X reveals is not … but …'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the Lima Registro de Predios keep handwritten ledger entries alongside digital records?
options: Because Peru's government has not yet funded a full digital system
Because handwritten entries show every correction as a visible scar, making alteration traceable
Because digital entries are considered less secure from hacking
Because the handwritten entries are easier to read than digital ones
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay states that a handwritten entry 'shows every correction as a visible scar', making it more transparent than a digital entry that 'can be altered without leaving a physical trace'.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay contrasts what happens when each type of record is amended.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a perfect participle clause to express a prior encoding?
options: Having encoded every tremor, pressure point, and habitual loop into its strokes, handwriting becomes a kind of biometric deposit.
Encoding every tremor and habitual loop, handwriting is becoming a biometric deposit.
Handwriting having encoded tremors into strokes, it becomes a biometric kind of deposit.
Every tremor encoded into strokes, handwriting is a biometric deposit having become.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Having encoded …, handwriting becomes …' is a correct perfect participle clause: prior action compressed into a clause whose subject matches the main clause subject.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Having + past participle' as the opener, same subject as the main clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'biometric deposit' mean as used in the essay?
options: A bank account linked to a fingerprint scanner
A physical record that stores the unique bodily movements of the person who wrote it
A digital file storing a person's health data
A scientific measurement of handwriting speed
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay describes handwriting as encoding 'tremor, pressure point, and habitual loop' — unique to each body — making it a physical deposit of biometric information.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Biometric' relates to the unique physical characteristics of a body.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'The body is made to vouch for the mind's intention'?
options: Our physical health determines how well we can think.
A handwritten signature requires the body to physically commit to an intention in a way that cannot be easily separated from the person.
The mind controls all bodily movements including handwriting.
Digital signatures are more accurate than handwritten ones.
mcQuestions.explanation: By signing, a person's physical movements (the body) are used to confirm a mental act of consent (the mind's intention) — the body literally enacts the mind's commitment.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Vouch for' means to confirm or guarantee.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction shows that handwriting remains required by law?
options: remain the legally required form of consent
the law requires handwriting as the form of consent
handwriting requires the law to have consent form
legally, consent is remaining required in handwriting form
mcQuestions.explanation: 'The legally required form of consent' uses a past participle as a pre-noun modifier ('required'), implying something has been required by law — a compressed passive.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'required' used as a modifier before a noun.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'irreproducibility' mean in the context of this essay?
options: The inability of handwriting to be read clearly
The property of handwriting that makes each instance unique and impossible to exactly copy
The slowness of handwriting compared to typing
The tendency of handwriting to fade over time
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay concludes that 'what we trust in handwriting is … its irreproducibility' — the property that prevents any two instances from being identical.
mcQuestions.hint: The prefix 'ir-' means 'not'; 'reproducible' means 'able to be exactly copied'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What argument does the essay concede in favour of digital authentication?
options: Digital records are better at preserving emotional content.
Digital records are faster, more secure against forgery, and avoid human error in handwritten records.
Digital records are required by international law for all contracts.
Digital records are preferred by most people under thirty-five.
mcQuestions.explanation: The concession paragraph acknowledges that cryptographic keys are 'orders of magnitude harder to replicate' and that human error in handwritten records 'is well documented'.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at the third paragraph, which begins with 'Granted'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'not only … but …' to extend an argument?
options: what we want from authenticity is not only security but presence
what we want from authenticity is not only security but also
not only security but what we want from authenticity is presence
security and presence not only but authenticity we want
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Not only X but Y' adds a second, often more important element. Here 'presence' is the deeper, less obvious need the essay argues for.
mcQuestions.hint: The correct structure is 'not only [noun] but [noun]'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Why does the essay mention the 1945 Instrument of Surrender in Tokyo Bay?
options: To argue that international law should use digital signatures
To show that even at the most significant historical moments, handwritten signatures were required
To illustrate that Japan had no digital records in 1945
To provide a military history example unrelated to handwriting
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay uses the 1945 Instrument of Surrender to illustrate that at a historically momentous moment, handwritten signatures were insisted upon — 'a form that no digital stamp could have replicated'.
mcQuestions.hint: Consider why the essay places this example in the 'persistence in ceremonial contexts' paragraph.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'renders everything uniform' mean in the essay's final paragraph?
options: Print makes text easier to read by standardising letter shapes
Print destroys the individual trace and makes all text look the same
Print requires more effort to produce than handwriting
Print produces more authentic documents than handwriting
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Renders everything uniform' means print removes all individual variation, making it impossible to distinguish one writer from another — the opposite of handwriting's irreproducibility.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Uniform' means identical in appearance — no variation.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which opening sentence best grounds the essay's argument in a specific, concrete case?
options: In today's digital world, handwriting is increasingly seen as old-fashioned.
It is a well-known fact that people still use handwriting for important documents.
At Lima's Registro de Predios — Peru's land title registry — property transactions have been authenticated by handwritten ledger entries for over a century.
Throughout history, humans have used writing as a way to communicate and record important events.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C opens with a specific institution, a specific country, a specific function, and a specific duration — grounding the abstract argument immediately in a concrete case.
mcQuestions.hint: Specific institution + country + function + time period beats any generic opener.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best pivots back to the main argument after the concession?
options: Furthermore,
For example,
Even so,
On the other hand,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' acknowledges the validity of the conceded point while asserting that the main argument still holds — the standard pivot after a B2 concession.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Even so' = 'despite all that, my point stands'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase from the essay describes how a document made by hand is received differently from one that is produced mechanically?
options: ordered in magnitude
biometric deposit
a record of time spent, pressure applied, and attention given
the mind's intention
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A record of time spent, pressure applied, and attention given' captures the human presence that distinguishes handwritten documents from typed or printed ones.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a phrase in the fourth paragraph that describes what a handwritten letter 'arrives as'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains a formal B2 register?
options: Honestly, handwriting is way more trustworthy than typing and everyone knows it.
Like, signatures are obviously important and digital stuff can't replace them.
The persistence of handwriting in legal and ceremonial contexts suggests that what we want from authenticity is not only security but presence.
Handwriting is trusted more than print and this is a really interesting topic tbh.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses precise vocabulary ('persistence', 'ceremonial', 'authenticity') and an analytical, measured tone appropriate for B2.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay predict about the value of handwriting's irreproducibility in the future?
options: It will become legally obsolete as digital verification improves.
It will become less important as people write by hand less often.
It may become one of the most valuable properties a document can possess in a world of generated text.
It will remain important only in Peru and Japan.
mcQuestions.explanation: The final sentence says: 'In a world increasingly populated by generated text, that refusal may turn out to be among the most valuable properties any document can possess.'
mcQuestions.hint: Read the final sentence of the essay carefully.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of the last time you received something handwritten — a note, a card, a letter. Did it feel different from a typed message with the same words? What exactly was different, and why do you think that difference existed?
